name: meter
title: Meter
description: A labelled gauge for a static measured value within a known range (disk usage, budget spent, a score). Built on Base UI Meter with role=meter semantics — distinct from Progress's role=progressbar, which tracks task advancement over time. Optional threshold segments (low/optimal/high) always pair color with a visible text label and a track tick, so the coloring is never the only cue.
useWhen: Displaying a current measured level within a known range (disk usage, budget spent, a score, capacity used)
The number is a snapshot reading of a static or slowly-changing quantity, not a task's completion
You want threshold-based coloring (low/optimal/high) that stays readable without color (forced-colors, color-blindness)
avoidWhen: Tracking a task's advancement toward completion over time, such as an upload or import (use Progress — role=progressbar)
An indeterminate wait with no measurable value yet (use Progress's indeterminate state or a Spinner)
The number has no meaningful min/max range to gauge against (use plain text or a Badge)

keyboard: The meter is a status indicator and never takes focus
Exposes role=meter with aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax, and aria-valuetext
Unlike Progress, a Meter has no indeterminate state — a static measurement is always a known value when it is shown
The value is paired with visible label/value text so meaning never relies on the fill color or fill length alone
notes: role=meter (not role=progressbar) tells assistive tech this is a static gauge, not task progress — screen readers announce the two differently, which is the whole reason both components exist.
Threshold segments always render two non-color cues alongside the tone: the active segment's name as visible text next to the value, and a tick mark in the track at each threshold boundary.

Track and indicator both keep a visible border (forced-colors:border-[CanvasText]) so the filled portion stays legible when Windows High Contrast overrides background colors and the track/indicator would otherwise resolve to the same system color.
The indicator's inline width is anchored with Base UI's inset-inline-start positioning, so the fill grows from the inline-start edge and mirrors correctly in RTL with no extra CSS.
Width transitions are gated behind motion-safe via transition-[width] motion-reduce:transition-none.
